It was informative, fun, and productive. Financially, we’ve decreased our deficit. The best thing we can do to improve the bottom line is to have more members, more rides and more riders. If you haven’t renewed for 2010, please do so. And get a friend to join! There are several rule change proposals. The full text of these can be seen in the Hoof Print. PLEASE talk about these with others and with your R1 Board of Di- rectors (Phil Young, Jim Ferris, Angie and Steve Meroshnekoff, Linda Thomason, Stephanie Swain, Gene Boicelli, Jamie Dieterich and Sue Flagg. The process is that new proposals are presented at the February National Board meeting, the wording is finalized (or completely rejected) at the July meeting, and they are voted on at the November meeting. We welcome your comments all through the process. There could also be some proposals for changes in the bylaws concerning how elections for our national board representatives are held. Again – wording will be finalized at the July meeting. Changes in the bylaws are voted onby the entire membership, so you’ll have a direct say after the final wording is decided. Our national sponsor chair has done a FANTASTIC job of getting sponsors for us. Please see the list elsewhere in this issue. Show your support by buying from them, and tell them “thanks” for being a sponsor of NATRC. Go to the R1 web site for links to their web sites. This is the 50 th year for NATRC, and our actual 50 th anniversary will be celebrat- ed at next year’s national convention in Nashville, TN, February 10-13. Meanwhile, our national Marketing Committee has been busy developing ways to make us more visible and recognized. The National BOD approved a newer, simplified logo (shown below). It is essentially what we’ve had before, but simplified to not include the map in the background. More information and files you can use will be posted on the NATRC web site for your convenience. A newly formed Technology Committee is developing a commercial subscriber- based mass email marketing system by Constant Contact to send news to different groups within NATRC. We’ll get information to you when it’s ready to go. Respectfully submitted, Jamie Dieterich National Board of Directors Meeting Report 2010 Region 1 General Meeting Report Since there was no Secretary present, this report is short and, of necessity, limit- ed to my feeble memory. This meeting was held on 2-21-10. Phil Young called the meeting to order shortly after the Region 1 Awards breakfast. There were roughly 30 brave souls in attendance, including most of the Region 1 Board Members. The first order of business was to announce the results of the annual election. All of those running were elected. We have two new regular BOD Directors, Gene Boicelli and Stephanie Swain, both of whom are regular competitors. The one return- ing Director was Phil Young. It was also announced that, in a prior National election this year, Sue Flagg was elected as one of our Dual Directors (ones that serve on both the National BOD and the R1 BOD), replacing Lynne Hutnick. Jim Ferris was appointed at the National BOD meeting on the previous Thursday to serve out the remainder of Sue’s Alternate Dual Director term. The next order of business was an abbreviated financial report by the Treasurer, Jim Ferris. The Region still has a positive balance sheet. Further detail is elsewhere in this Leadline. Phil Young then gave a report on meetings and discussions the R1 BOD had dur- ing the year regarding the direction they felt NATRC was heading and suggested changes. Those suggestions included, but were not limited to, providing more differ- ence in pace and distance between the Divisions while staying within the current rules and providing a graduation in awards quality from Open to Novice, both for Ride and Year-end awards. After a lengthy but lively discussion, the continued on next page . . . The Leadline Spring 2010 3 It was great to be at the NATRC National Convention in Reno this year As always, it was so fun to put names to faces from other Regions, and reconnect with old friends. The orga- nizers did a fabulous job. Holding the convention in conjunction with AERC is al- ways a great idea as we get access to a great trade show and excellent speakers. Hands down the most facinating speaker was Jim Moore, with his animated models of the horse's gut to explain the mysteries of colic. No one who attended that talk will ever look at their horse the same way again! Another excellent speaker was Rick Potts of the National Park Service. Here was our opportunity to interact with an in- spirational man who is a fellow equestrian, who also happens to be in charge of the National Trails System. Wow! The really sad thing, however, was that Rick's two talks were both very sparsely attended. I mean, the speaker on horse nutrition had the place packed, but very few people bothered to hear about trails. I think this is a pretty sad statement about where a lot of people's priorities are. TRAILS are what we are about! TRAIL is the "T" in NATRC! There is no "H" in NATRC. I know a lot of horse people think that preserving trails is someone's else's job, but some day a lot of us are going to find our trails closed. You may not feel like getting involved until it's YOUR trail that is threatened, but you can at least take advan- tage of an opportunity to hear a knowledgeable individual talk about trail preservation. You can hang on to some of those ideas for when you need them down the road. You can enjoy Rick's beautiful pictures of packing in the Bob Marshall Wilder- ness. I hope that next time we have someone to speak about saving our trails, you will at least take the time to listen. Those who did not hear Rick Potts missed out. I hope there will be a next time." Thanks! Helen Steenman Letter to the Editor, Outreach/Publicity News During the ride season, the Outreach person or Outreach helpers try to make first time competitors feel welcome to the sport of Competitive Trail and the NATRC family. We do this by visiting the first time rider campsites to say “hello”, to answer questions and provide assistance in whatever way we can. New rider “welcome bags” are given out as well as an NATRC mug for the first time competitor high point horse/rider combi- nation. Not all members are aware that there is someone at most rides to assist first time competitors. Please help new rid- ers in whatever way you can but be aware that there usually is someone they can be directed to who can address their questions/concerns. If you find yourself parked next to a first time competitor, please make sure to ask who the Outreach person is for the ride (usually me or Sherry Lindley) and see that someone visits their campsite to offer assistance or just a welcome. If you’re a ride manager/ride secretary, please try to let the Outreach person at your ride know who the first time competi- tors are so that they can be welcomed. This information as- sists the Outreach person to keep track of the ride results and make sure that the first time high point rider/horse “ NATRC Mug” is given. I would like to take this opportunity to thank Sherry Lindley for offering to assist me in doing Outreach at the rides this year. Sherry has volunteered in numerous capacities this past year and deserves a big ThankYou. Here’s to a great ride season. See you out on the trail. Submitted by Phil Young . . . continued from previous page Return to the Livermore Wine Country for the 2011 Region One Mini-Convention Having just braved the snow to attend the Reno conven- tion, you’re probably saying “What ?? Convention?? When?? As your friendly Convention Chairperson for 2011 I’m putting the bug in your ear for March, 2011 as we head back to the Bothwell Art Center and the Livermore Wine Country. The lo- cation, venue and hotel worked out well in 09 and the goal is an even more fun weekend in 2011. So friends, start thinking about suggestions for speakers, vendors, raffle items.... If you come across anything in the next few months that might be useful for convention, PLEASE let me know. During the next 10 months, please talk to local stores/manufacturers and help obtain raffle prizes etc. Let me know if you’ve heard a dynamic speaker and think this person might be suitable for our group. This is a great opportunity for new comers to pitch in and help the organization. Putting on a convention takes a lot effort and time (and Advil, etc) and we need the support of all members. Thanks in advance!! For those of you who live in the Livermore area, please note that the Livermore museum on Main Street is presenting an exhibit through Spring and Summer focused on the contri- butions of the horse to the Livermore Valley. Linda Thomason 2011 Convention Chair linda.thomason@comcast.net Convention Silent Auction and Raffle from Betty Young I want to thank all of you who donated items for the Silent Auction and the raffle that took place at our National Conven- tion in Reno. Because of your generosity, and all the great things we had to offer, the auction netted $2,008.55, which in- cludes $250 earmarked for the Region 1 Junior Fund, and the raffle took in $735.50, for a grand total of $2,744.05, That amount will, I’m sure, make a big difference in the financial bot- tom line for the Convention. Many Convention attendees commented on the wonderful array of items we had for the Silent Auction. Thanks again to everyone for coming through in such grand style! Quarry Helen Steenman – Georgetown Laurie Knuutila - Challenge of the North Kathryn Lewandowsky - Pilchuck Steve Meroshnekoff – Jackson Forest Chere Barger - Harvey Bear Ranch Joe Pimentel – Oakland Hills/R1 Benefit Ride Gene Boicelli – Round Valley Region One Appreciation Certificates Jean Armer by Angie Meroshnekoff Many riders who find themselves unable to ride their horse for a time due to an injury usually take advantage of that time to do something outside of NATRC. Not this recip- ient. Finding herself sidelined from surgery, she continued to go to every ride that season knowing that she could fill in and help wherever needed. This past year she was well enough to ride but found her horse wasn’t, so once again, she volunteered her services at all the rides. While none of us can remember quite how long, she has been a member of NATRC for a very, very long time. She is a person with a generous spirit and is willing to give her time, knowledge or support to anyone in need. She can be counted on to offer a hand where needed or loan you any equipment you left at home She is an experienced judge’s secretary and has been an impromptu ride secretary. She’s often found herself as ride timer, fill in cook, new rider buddy, P&R person and general gofer. She chaired the Region 1 Mini-Convention for several years, a project which is a huge undertaking. Jean Armer, you have gone way above and beyond for us and your Region really appreciates you! The Leadline Spring 2010 5 NATRC Region 1 2009 Awards Presentation, continued Lee Canon by Steve Meroshnekoff Lee showed up at a ride several years ago at the P/R check in as a new volunteer. Lee had been around horses a lot, but had no real experience with NATRC, so I made him assistant timer. Lee figured out that game very fast. One day I asked Lee if he could do P/R checks. Well, I got the same answer I usually get “I don’t know about this.” I hooked him up with an experienced teammate, and before long I had to break Lee off a team and place him on his own. One day at the Jackson Forest Ride, I needed a driver for a judge, but we had no vehicle to drive around. Coming to my rescue, he unhooked his truck from his horse trailer and became a judge’s driver not knowing what he was get- ting into (the terrain at this ride is pretty darn tough and the driver’s map was not the best). It seems like every ride now is short a driver, so I do a search for Lee’s campsite where I hear “I figured you would be looking for me, What do you need now?” with a big smile on his face. We deeply appreciate Lee’s becoming a reliable “Judge’s Taxi”. Brian Schlerf by Steve Meroshnekoff I met Brian when his daughter started riding at our ranch about six months before my second ride at Jackson Forest. I learned Brian loves to barbeque, so I asked him if he could help out at as a cook at the ride. Brian said “Yup I can do that.” Job title didn’t matter to Brian, so there he was helping setting up the kitchen area, the lighting, the tables and organizing the kitchen trailer. After the first meal was served, Brian was heading out of camp to help with the “road crossing” as a crossing guard. Later that morning Bri- an was spotted cleaning up manure and helping fill water tanks in camp. He said he hated standing around. After the ride Brian helped with breaking down the kitchen area, loading up all the equipment, securing all the lights, clean- ing up all the manure in the camp and hauling a truckload of stuff back to my ranch. Brian came along and offered to help at the next ride while his daughter, Haley, rode. It wasn’t long before Brian was running the timing at the P&R’s, loaning out his truck for driving the judges around, and doing anything else that needed to be done. THANK YOU, Brian, for all you do! Given in memo- ry of our late, much loved Max, it primarily honors those who have worked “in the trenches” at our Region 1 rides for many years. This year’s recipient is Linda Thomason, who has worn many different hats throughout her years of involvement with NATRC. She has been our Mini-Convention Secretary in both 2002 and 2003; she has been the region’s Publicity Chairman, including for the 2004 National Convention that we hosted here in Reno; and she was the Chairman and one-woman show for last year’s Mini-Convention in Livermore. She is also currently serving on the Region 1 Board of Directors. However, today we’re spotlighting all the jobs she has filled at Region 1 rides. Linda has often driven hundreds of miles and taken countless vacation days from work just to be at our rides as a volunteer—on P&R teams, as a Judge’s Sec- retary and as Rules Interpreter. But perhaps she most shines in her role as Region 1’s Hostess with the Mostest. She is al- ways so warm and welcoming to new riders, answering their questions, offering suggestions and presenting them with gift bags filled with goodies, purchased at her own expense. At the end of the ride day, she walks through camp with trays of cheese, salami and crackers for riders and workers alike. If the Judges and their Secretaries are still working on scoring cards when dinner is served, she makes sure they have meals brought to them. In short, whatever needs to be done at a ride, she is there to do it......always gracious and smiling and always promoting our sport. We are enormously grateful for her and wish to offer her our sincerest thanks. I am very honored to be able to pres- ent Linda with the Max Barmettler Honorary Lifetime Award today, and to say.......HATS OFF TO YOU my friend!! Max Barmettler Award Honorary Region 1 Lifetime Membership Linda Thomason by Betty Young NATRC Student Loan Program Student loans are available up to $5,000 per person /year, at the discretion of the NATRC National Board of Directors, after proper application has been submitted. Preference will be given to a full-time student who has been a member of NATRC or whose family has been an active member for at least three years. Consideration will also be given to a student or a student’s family who has been an active member of NATRC for less than three years but more than six months. A loan will be made based upon financial need, scholastic achievement, and character of the applicant as determined by the Student Loan/Scholarship Committee. A loan is to be repaid in monthly installments, with an annu- al interest rate of 3% charged on that loan, beginning not later than six months following graduation or when the recipient ceases to be a full-time student. For anapplication, contact the NATRC Executive Adminis- trator at natrc@natrc.org Please complete the form in Word and submit via email to the NATRC Executive Administrator at natrc@natrc.org NATRC Student Scholarship Program Student scholarships are available for individuals planning a career in equine related fields. Up to $5,000 will be available annually with distribution at the discretion of the NATRC Na- tional Board of Directors. Applicants need to complete theBev Tibbitts Memorial Scholarshipform as well as submit three (3) letters of support for your scholarship candidacy. The Leadline Spring 2011 1 In This Issue.... Chairman’s Corner 2 Convention 2011 4 Region 1 Awards 5 End of Year Placings 7 NBOD Meeting Report 8 Use of Whips 8 New Hoof Boot Rule 9 New Drug Rule 9 Ride Calendar 10 Reg. 1 BOD Meeting Report 11 The Leadline Spring 2011 The Voice of NATRC Region 1 Competitive Trail Riding www.natrcregion1.org It all started on a ride my husband and I took in the early fall of 2008. It was like a cartoon image, you know the one, a light bulb suddenly appears above the charac- ter’s head and an idea is formed. My husband said, “Wouldn’t it be great if I could put on a ride in our Mt. Diablo foothills here.” I suddenly fell silent, fortunately I did not gasp. You see many, many, years ago I had chaired the Mt, Diablo ride when Gene was not involved with N.A.T.R.C. I was volunteering at the time in some facet, being Secretary, P&R person, etc. for the rides on the mountain. At that time we were busy working and raising our children, so my volunteering was considered a fun time for me and he did not remember me pulling my hair out after biting off the chairmanship. At that time there were usually 60 or more riders on these rides, so even though it was a well established ride it was an awesome task. Now Gene was in an excited frame of mind considering which trails we could use and where we could stage. He exclaimed, “I could be the trail master.” Thus the organism was spawned. My horse and I took some deep breaths as we rode along and I acknowledged Gene’s enthusi- asm with some “uhuhs.” I fully realized all the work that would be involved even as trail master. The organism was gaining strength as the days progressed. It was fueled by ideas at this point in time. “Round Valley would be a great staging area, don’t you think”? There was that light bulb again. “We should get a hold of Sue Flagg. She rides that area all the time.” “We can connect it to the trails on our side of the canyon.” I pondered, when did “I “ turn into “we?” Sue was interested in the idea so we rode out together to investigate the feasibility of the trails. The organism gained more ground. Sue suddenly stated, “If you chair I’ll be your Secretary.” Gene kind of hesitated for a moment and said, “Wait a moment, I was going to be trail master.” Sue chimed in,” Well, you can do both.” I did not say a word but somehow I knew right then I would be along for another kind of ride. At least I thought there are plenty of good volunteers that could lend Gene a hand and Sue had lots of experience as to what needed to be done. Thank god for that. Over the weeks and months of planning, the organism stretched itself and began a feeding frenzy on paper of all kinds. Permits and copies of each. Post-It notes of numerous colors, envelopes, many maps and, of course, copies of such strewn in every conceivable place. Refuge was to be found only in the bathroom. Dessert to this rather hungry demon became bags full of bright colored ribbons with clothespins attached. I wondered, would my little house ever be the same? I envied any home neat and organized and avoided them. Sue, Gene and I would have lunch meetings together. Sue, like me, had a thing for Mexican food. Which was a good thing. We would discuss the organism behind its back and work on taming it. I know Gene and Sue got tired of me asking, “Have we done this and have we done that?” but I had a need to know that this thing we had created was staying on track. Fortunately what one person didn’t think of the other one did. Riding the ride three times and timing it was good for both riders and horses, a time free from work until we returned to write it all down. We tried to rideas if we had never seen the trails before and would ribbon accordingly. When it came to ribboning, we had terrific help. Our neighbor up the road, Chris Heath,ribboned on our canyon side. Sue ribboned on her side of Round Valley. It was about this time that The Road to Round Valley By Victoria Boicelli Continued on page 3 . . . Region 1 Veterinary Judge Dr. Carol Ormond, D.V.M., recipient of Region 1 Appreci- ation at the National Level The Leadline Spring 2011 2 I’d like to take a few lines to introduce myself as the 2011 Region One Chairperson. I wish to thank my fellow Board of Directors members for electing me to this position and welcome the opportunity to work for the benefit of NATRC. I’ve been an active member of NATRC for over 20 years both as a competitor and “in the background.” I’ve worked as ride secretary, rules interpreter, P&R volunteer, outreach chairperson, publicity chairperson and on multiple conventions, including the 2011 Livermore convention, which I hope you all enjoyed. I have a broad background in NATRC and believe in the organization’s goals of promoting horsemanship and good trail horses. I hope the coming year provides me the opportunity to meet new members and reunite with past members. The coming year presents some major challenges. Due to the escalating gas costs, competitors will face major financial challenges just driving to rides. I encourage all riders to “truck” pool. Get together with other riders in your area (check the member roster) and plan ahead to pool your resources. Don’t give up on rides simply because of the driving distance. Be innovative and think of ways riders can save money and pool resources. Then, share these ideas with your fellow riders by submitting a Leadline article. For instance: plan a vacation camping trip and incorporate a ride at the end or beginning of your week. Bring friends from your area to a ride and share the expenses. The Leadline belongs to YOU - become an active participant. Another challenge is that Bay Area parks are re-assessing what groups they need to provide for and equestrians are not high on the list. Attend park district meetings and support local trail groups that are fighting in Sacramento and Washington to ensure that all sports enthusiasts are included in the guidelines for trail use. Sitting on the sidelines simply means that some day you’ll have no where left to ride! I encourage everyone to become active – go out there and volunteer for trail repair days- andwear your NATRC t-shirts so that other workers are made aware of our organization and our support for multi-use trails. If interested, you can contact California Recreational Trails Committee and get on their mailing list at: CESSEX@parks.ca.gov Spring is in the air, the ride season is around the corner and I sincerely hope that all of you will be out there enjoying the trails. Continued from page 1 I realized things were really coming to- gether. Boxes now entered the picture but these were the treasures I had picked for prizes so even though there were many I tolerated them well. Volunteers were lined up and knew their positions like troops in the organism’s Army. What would we do without that good Army? Before long it was October. It was warm but not too warm and there was water to be found, a big plus. People started to arrive at the staging area and parked nicely in the space provided. The sounds of horses and their people sang an oh so familiar song and check in, al- ways fun to watch, went smoothly. The potluck on Friday night, as usual had plenty of palate appeal. People were chat- ting excitedly, anticipating the next day’s competition. Briefing went well and Gene came away relieved and as if he had had a triple shot Starbucks Latte. All was becoming calm in camp, horses were standing quietly, munching on hay, their people turning in for the night. The sound of “Taps” resonated from a CD we had and seemed to address the feel of camp at this time. I was more then ready for sleep but it was just not to be. I can sleep anywhere and if I know there is a job or competition the next day, I sleep and stockpile my adrenalin for the task at hand in the morning. It is not so for my husband and daughter. My daughter was riding her dad’s horse for him the next day. She proceeded to have the light on and read until about 2 a.m. Gene tossed and turned all night until I felt like I was playing a game of tennis. Between the two of them in that small trailer there was very little sleep to be found before the resounding tones of reveille were played. I had only at the most, 3 hours sleep to fall back on. My horse was strangely sedate as I saddled him, gazing off in the distance and standing like chiseled granite. Hum, I thought, this might be a good thing, since usually he is doing something that closely resembles the Jitterbug. I mounted him, still a rock statue. As soon as I settled into the saddle, I felt the muscles tense up under me and fully expected to hear “Gentlemen, start your engines” except for the fact that I believe his was already started. I knew there would be a horse- manship judge at the dry creek on the way out, it was a perfect place for one. I also knew that to try to hold back on my horse by much would be foolhardy. He was eager and animated. It wasn’t pretty, but I maintained some semblance of control and we were off and trotting. It was still half dark but I saw a helpful rider up ahead of me holding open the gate to Hardy Trail, and this is something I enjoy about N.A.T.R.C., riders helping out other riders when they see the need. It was time to test the organism. Had it been fed properly? The paper, maps, ribbons, were they all working within its frame with ener- gy and purpose? The morning was cool against my cheeks as that warming orb’s light tiptoed up the dead grass hills turning them to California gold. The Oaks stood as sentries overseeing our pathway as we went. My little horse gathered himself be- neath me with arched neck ready to go in any direction at the touch of my heel from 0 to 40 in a flash. He, as always, looks twice his size in this mode and was ready to conquer cities or at least slay a few dragons. As for me, I was enjoying every moment of it. Forget the lack of sleep, the adrenalin was on overdrive. Likewise the people we trotted with or passed were also enjoying themselves. At P&R stops and lunch, riders were smiling, laughing and having a good time. I began to see that maybe the organism wasn’t such a bad sort after all. The ride was somewhat like a wed- ding, much planning and preparation and then over before you know it. I got to hand out awards and give everyone a hug. The adrenalin was still going and tiredness was held at bay. Oh, and I forgot to men- tion, the dinner was Mexican food. Could I ask for more? Gene’s jaw had relaxed and he looked content with how every- thing had gone. All horses and riders had made it back to camp without problems. As always, riders had paid their utmost attention to the needs of their horses. For as Jamie Dieterich, horsemanship judge, said, “ It is, after all, all about the horse.” This is, of course, what brings us all to- gether, a love and respect for our four hoofed companions whose cadence car- ries us and causes our hearts to soar. The organism was packed up neatly in a large brown box and slept quietly throughout the winter. In early spring the box began to tremble and once again it was emerging. I must admit I greeted it with a better attitude this time. I would be more familiar with its needs. Gene was grinning with more ideas and even more enthusiasm to feed it. Ya just gotta love it! Our second year ride was a bit more more challenging because of the Indian Summer heat. So we will this year, 2011, be having a two day ride June 18 and 19. See you there! “Do Come ride with us.’” You are sure to enjoy the Round Valley Ride and who knows - the organism might per- suade you to start a ride of your own. Jump right in, the waters are filled with good people who will run with your ideas. We do need a few more rides. NATRC Student Loan Program Student loans are available up to $5,000 per person /year, at the discretion of the NATRC National Board of Directors, after proper application has been submitted. Preference will be given to a full-time student who has been a member of NATRC or whose family has been an active member for at least three years. Consideration will also be given to a student or a student’s family who has been an active member of NATRC for less than three years but more than six months. A loan will be made based upon financial need, scholastic achievement, and character of the applicant as determined by the Student Loan/Scholarship Committee. A loan is to be repaid in monthly installments, with an annual interest rate of 3% charged on that loan, beginning not later than six months following graduation or when the recipient ceases to be a full-time student. For anapplication, contact the NATRC Executive Administrator at natrc@natrc.org Please complete the form in Word and submit via email to the NATRC Executive Administrator at natrc@natrc.org Attention Junior Riders! The Region 1 Board of Directors voted to continue the Junior Rider Payback program at their October 2010 meeting. Junior riders can receive a payback of 30.00 per ride on a minimum of two rides and a maximum of three rides entered during the 2011 ride season. Contact Phil Young for more informa- tion. Raffle tickets for the Tevis Cup 100-mile ride entry are availa- ble. Must be qualified to en- ter. Drawing May 21, 2011. Contact Jamie Dieterich. The Leadline Spring 2011 4 I wish to thank all of you who “Returned to the Livermore Wine Country” for the 2011 Region One Convention. This was a fun event and I thank all the “helpers” who contribut- ed to its success. The Bothwell Art Center once again left up their Mardi Gras decorations to lend “décor” and the bright colors, mardi gras masks and paper mache characters that lined the auditorium lent a festive at- mosphere. We had a great assort- ment of vendors with the flagship being Healthy as a Horse Network. I must compliment Susan Favro (the owner of Healthy as a Horse) for con- tributing to our Region Silent Auction with her purchase of a beautiful sad- dle rack made by our very own crafts- man Lee Cannon. Thank you, Lee, for your generous donation of time and work to produce the saddle rack and bridle racks! The vendors had a won- derful variety of items. I hope you all enjoyed the shopping as much as I did! I was unable to listen to most of the speakers but the feedback from attendees was very positive. I recall walking through the speaker room in the morning and seeing the entire group practicing emergency wound wrapping. Kudos to Bruce and the paramedic group for providing all the gauze and “extras” for attendees to take back for their medical kits. Many members informed me how impressed they were with Dr. McKean’s presentation. Their first thought was “Oh, another chiropractic lecture”. However, as a rider who has overcome physical handicaps, his ap- proach was quite different and provid- ed much useful information. A big Thank You to Debbie Murphy for ar- ranging for Dr. McKean to speak! Becky Hart’s presentation fo- cused on what the U.S. Endurance Team has learned in the past 4 years in order to be competitive with many of the international groups that have overtaken the U.S. in endurance com- petition. The talk and pictures were quite interesting and entertaining. Becky also talked about the speeds being attained in these races: less than 8 hour 100’s are needed just to compete internationally and less than 7 hour 100’s are required to win. Dr. Sharon Spier provided useful tips and insights into pigeon fever in California. While this could be a dry topic, Dr. Spier is a delightful, enter- taining speaker and provided informa- tion in a way that kept our interest piqued. I would like to thank those that attended but basically “gave up” much of their day to help out and make the convention a success. First and fore- most I want to extend a heartfelt THANK YOU to Betty Young for her hard work as the convention secre- tary. Unless you’ve done this job, you just don’t realize how much work it is to keep the financials straight, make up the registration packets and an- swer everyone’s questions. Kudos to Betty for doing such a great job! Re- gion One also owes a big thank you to Kathy Nixon. She dug her way through the snow in Quincy to her storage shed to find tablecloths and containers for convention. She worked the breakfast and lunch table all day and then went on in the evening with Jamie Dietrich to host the award ceremony. Kathy is the person who orders all the year end awards – so if you enjoyed those rib- bons- thank her when you see her. Jamie and Kathy did another great job with the awards ceremony. During the year, we see Jamie at the rides snap- ping pictures of everyone. Well she does like photography but there’s an ulterior motive to her shutterbug ma- nia. At the year end awards, she has a picture of every rider to flash on the screen as they come up to accept their awards. Region One really owes a special thanks to Jamie Dieterich! When you see her at rides this sum- mer-SMILE-this may be a Kodak mo- ment for you! We had a huge raffle table with over 100 raffle items. The booming voice at the raffle table belonged to Gene Boicelli who did a great job selling tickets and keeping the raffle board up to date. Thank You Region One members who supported the raf- fle both by bringing items and pur- chasing tickets! The silent auction also had many great items. I must tell you that Marilyn Hunter’s “hooch cake” was again the most bid on item! Marilyn also brought a chocolate chip cheese cake which ended up being “sold” to the highest bidder at the awards dinner. Thank you to Donna Stidolph for helping to man the lunch bench and keeping it organized and stocked with Kathy Nixon’s help; to Bob and Karel Waugh who continue to come early to help set up on Friday and stay late on Saturday to clean up There were oth- ers who helped straighten up Satur- day night- thanks so much I know I’ve forgotten to thank someone, so please know that I really appreciate all of you who help out with these events. For those of you who are new to NATRC, convention is a fun time to meet up with friends after the long winter and get revved up for the coming ride season. It’s a lot of fun- so if you haven’t gone in the past- give it a try next time. In 2012, the Region One con- vention will join with Region Two and AERC in Reno. Information will be available later in the year as the plans are finalized. We are seeking volun- teers for the 2013 convention- so if you have ideas- step up and jump in. I especially encourage new members/riders to participate and help the organization. We need fresh ideas and enthusiasm to keep the spirit alive and kicking! I’ve rambled on too long AGAIN! Convention was a success, the awards dinner was a gourmet event, the wine was plentiful- and now we’re ready to move on and prepare for a great ride season. Robyn Berwickby Linda Thomason Robyn has been an active member of NATRC for over 20 years. When you search out Robyn at rides, you look for the lady with a German Shepherd at her heels and a big welcoming smile on her face. She is a firefighter with the city of Modesto and when she’s not riding, she loves to travel to far reaching places such as the Arctic Circle and the Galapagos Islands. She is a woman of many talents. She lives in a log house in Mountain Ranch, which she built herself! Robyn started competing in NATRC with her horse, Domino. When Domino retired, Robyn began competing on Boone and currently has two Missouri Fox Trotters, Dundee and Mingo She has re- cruited many new members to the sport and has been generous with her time in mentoring new riders as well as loaning them horses to ride! Robyn has served as secretary for Region One and is now the Ride Chairperson for the Arnold Rim Trail Ride. She is always helping others. If her horse was pulled at a ride, Robyn would jump in and help out for the rest of the ride in whatever capacity she could. Robyn is also known for her extraor- dinary talents as a raffle announcer at rides. Thank You, Robyn, for your time, your generosity, and for the caring and dedication you have shown to NATRC. We appreciate all your efforts! The Leadline Spring 2011 7 Region One Awards Presentation, continued . . . Region One Special Awards Bob Smith Award High Point 1st Year Novice or CP horse Virga BL ridden by Ashley Dillard (CP) Pat Ersepke Award High Point Novice Horse/Rider Combo Tango Sierra ridden by Laura Harvey Fran & Carol Duport Award High Point 1st Year Open Horse Lady Tigers Hysyn ridden by Haley Schlerf Max Barmettler Award Honorary Region 1 Lifetime Membership Chere Bargar by Betty Young One of Region 1’s most special annual awards is the Max Barmettler Hon- orary Regional Lifetime Award. Given in memory of our late, much loved Max, it primarily honors those who have worked “in the trenches” at our Region 1 rides for many years. I am so pleased and honored to be able to present this award tonight to my good friend, Chere Bargar. Perhaps many of you don’t know Chere all that well, but she has been involved with Region 1 and National NATRC for years....practically forever, in fact....but she manages to somehow stay in the background. She was the National Secretary from 1979 to 1984.and she has been a very proactive volunteer for many trail issues and organizations, including the Henry Coe State Park mounted patrol. Tonight, however, we’re mainly sa- luting the many efforts she has made to directly help with our rides. No one worked harder than Chere on the Gilroy Ride for all those countless years. She spent weeks scouting out those trails we all loved---she pruned them, marked them, timed them, rode point, rode safety and sometimes did both. How’s that for being a jack-of-all-trades? She was also invaluable for so many years helping with the trails on the Almaden Valley Ride, performing the same myriad of duties she did for Gilroy. When the Gilroy Ride sadly ceased to exist a few years ago, Chere must have decided she was bored (in spite of all her other volunteerism) and so she put on her own ride, the Harvey Bear Ranch Ride, in 2009 and 2010 to benefit one of her fa- vorite local charities. Truth be told, Chere is one of the hardest working people I’ve ever known and also one of the most self-effacing. She never seeks any credit for all the back-breaking hours she spends---on a horse, in a truck and even on a bicycle--- making our rides possible. Tonight we not only applaud Chere for over 30 years of dedication to our sport, we also give her our sincerest THANKS!! 5 th TA Mira-Image / Arab / Stephanie Swain Novice Junior Horse 1st My Gal Sal / Grad / Karlie Johnson 2 nd Chi / Must / Kaysha Isgrigg The Leadline Spring 2011 8 National Board of Directors Meeting Report The meeting was held in Nashville, TN, February 10, 2011. First, I’d like to thank everyone for supporting me in another term to serve you on the National Board. It is very rewarding and fulfilling to work with such a great team of people in this terrific sport that means so much to me. Alternate NBOD: Phil Young was approved for appointment to be Region 1’s Alternate National Board represent- ative to fill the remainder of Jim Ferris’ term. Business of NATRC: NATRC end- ed 2010 with a shortfall of $15,000. This was largely driven by a drop in revenue due to a decrease in membership, rides and riders. Although we are still financially stable, deficit spending is not a good way to go. An increase in dues was approved at the November, 2010 meeting. Due to the huge success of the Specialized Sad- dle raffle, a decision will be made at next July’s meeting for a new national fund- raising raffle. Region 1’s goals to help increase revenues for 2011 include in- creasing our number of rides (6 in 2010) by 10% (we have 6 scheduled for 2011 plus the AK ride) and increasing the aver- age number of riders per ride (24 in 2010). To help cut expenses, the Hoof Print lay- out and design was put out for bid. The contract was awarded to Kelly Carper Communications, Missoula, Montana. A final budget for 2011 was approved. Marketing and Publicity:Articles appeared in several other publications, including one in Trail Blazer magazine (see www.natrc.org). E-News, a subscrib- er based email application administered by Bev Roberts, R4, is delivering informa- tion very efficiently. NATRC has a page on FaceBook, administered by Chuck Smith of R3. Jonni Jewell was interviewed for an hour on Rick Lamb’s radio show in Janu- ary. Sponsors:Please remember to buy from and say thanks to our wonderful national sponsors. See the Region 1 or national web sites for more information. Drug Testing and Rules: More rides outside of CA will be tested. A com- prehensive drug rule was passed at last November’s meeting, but the penalty phase was put under further review. A special committee came back with pro- posals offering specific and strict penal- ties. After much discussion, it was felt a penalty statement allowing for more dis- cretion would be more appropriate. See separate report on new rule proposals and an article in the Spring Hoof Print. Trails:A grant was awarded to repair a foot bridge on a segment of trail used by the Colorado Trail Competitive Trail Ride. Scholarships:A small scholarship was awarded to Jennifer Maddox, R4. Judges Committee: New judges are in the apprentice process. Concerns the committee is hearing have shifted from contrived obstacles to riders wanting judges to be more informative. What does “OK” mean? The committee reaffirmed the importance of all judges providing a qualified evaluation of all horses and rid- ers. Membership:Personal contact with prospective new members is very important. Proposed rule changes:See sep- arate report in Hoof Print. Proposed bylaws changes: The ballot for proposed bylaws changes was included in the Winter Hoof Print, but un- fortunately it seemed to fall out of many. Ballots will be distributed by each NBOD representative to their respective regions, a PDF will be posted on the national web site, and an announcement will be sent out in E-News. Members are urged to vote – it does matter. Because this was originally a 2010 ballot, 2010 members are eligible to vote. Rules Interpreters: A training presentation has been prepared for peo- ple interested in being a rules interpreter, and I will be happy to answer questions. Electronic Meetings: In an effort to improve efficiency and perhaps eventually to save costs, the National BOD will be conducting electronic meetings during the year. This service, GoToWebinar/ GoTo Meeting is also available to all the regions. Region 5 did an absolutely awe- some job of hosting the50 th Anniversary celebration at the 2011 National Con- vention. See up Spring Hoof Print for more reports! 2012 National Convention will be hosted by Region 2 in conjunction with the AERC Convention in Reno, NV March 9-10. Respectfully submitted, Jamie Dieterich Many of you have probably heard that if you want to use a whip at the final vet check-out, you had to have used it at the first vet check-in. The rationale for that was consistency in presentation. This guideline was in a document called theBOD Guidelines. These guidelines were a compilation of things that didn’t have a good “home.” Many people, including judges, didn’t know this document existed. All of the con- tents in this document have been moved to manuals (Judges, Manage- ment) where they belong, incorporated into rule changes, put into a new docu- ment calledPolicies and Procedures,or deleted. Thus the use of a whip at check-in was never a rule and was always diffi- cult to enforce fairly. The important thing is whether or not the handler uses a whip appropriately. A whip should really be just an extension of the arm. The veterinary judge needs to see the horse move freely to evaluate way of going, MAW (movement, attitude and willing- ness) and soundness. The handler needs to present the horse to meet those goals and to maintain control. For many horses, just seeing the whip in the handler’s hand encourages them to be- have better or move along better. The horsemanship judge should evaluate if the handler is using the whip appropriately. The veterinary judge will evaluate the horse’s movement, and he/she will not be fooled into thinking a truly tired horse is lively and animated. Use of a Whip at Check-In Good Search - Pennys for NATRC GoodSearch.com is a new search engine that donates half its revenue, about a penny per search, to the chari- ties its users designate. You use it just as you would any search engine, and it's powered by Yahoo! Just go toGoodSearch.com and be sure to enter NATRC as the charity you want to support. Just 500 of us searching four times a day will raise about $7,300 in a year without anyone spending a dime! And, be sure to spread the word! Also, GoodSearch has a toolbar which you may download and install on your browser. It is a clean, useful tool- bar which makes it easy to use Good- Search and with NATRC as your charity selection for Internet searches. The Leadline Spring 2011 9 New Hoof Boot Rule New Drug Rule With the help and approval of AERC, NATRC has adopted a new drug rule. Following a vote of the Board of Directors in November, 2010, the NATRC Rule Book will now have a sec- tion (Section 7) devoted entirely to ex- plaining the rules regarding prohibited substances and treatments. The pur- pose of these rules “in equines during NATRC rides is both to protect the equines from harm and to ensure fair competition. Equines should compete under their natural abilities without the influence of any drug, medication or vet- erinary treatment.” In addition, a set of detailed Ap- pendices will be maintained on the NATRC web site and updated yearly. Appendix A lists prohibited substances, Appendix B lists categories of prohibited substances, Appendix C gives catego- ries of allowed substances, Appendix D – published threshold levels, Appendix E – known detection times, Appendix F – prohibited treatments, and Appendix G – allowed treatments. Note: The Appendices are very similar, but not identical to AERC’s. For example, the use of ice in wraps or boots, Regumate to control estrus in mares, antacids, alcohol applied for cool- ing, and liniments without prohibited sub- stances are allowed in AERC rides but not in NATRC rides.The detail in these Appendices should greatly help veteri- narians, veterinary judges and riders know what is acceptable and what is not. At the November National Board meeting, some directors were concerned that the proposed new penalty wording was too general. A special committee came back to the February meeting with proposals offering specific and strict pen- alties. After much discussion, the Board felt a penalty statement allowing for more discretion would be more appropriate. It is recognized that not all offenses are the same; some might be accidental expo- sure, while some might be deliberate therapeutic administration. The penalty needs to fit the crime. The proposed wording would be: 1.Any rider/owner violating these rules at an NATRC ride shall forfeit any com- pletion or placing for the ride. NATRC may impose additional penalties for vio- lation of these rules on any person re- sponsible for the violation. Normally, the rider and owner shall be considered the persons responsible for the custody and care at a ride. Accordingly, the rider and owner shall avoid liability for additional penalties for violation of this rule only by showing by clear and convincing evi- dence that: (a) some other person outside of the rider or owner’s control was re- sponsible for the violation and (b) the rider or owner bore no fault for the violation. We are very grateful to AERC for all their help and cooperation. The new hoof boot rule is in effect for the 2011 ride year. The new rule reads as follows (changes in bold): Section 5.E.Hoof Protection 1. There are no shoeing restrictions. 2. All types of hoof boots that provide sole protection are allowed. However, any attached strap, keeper, or gaiter must not extend above the pastern. The judges may request to observe the area covered by the attached strap, keeper, or gaiter. Section 5.F.4. Protective devices, such as bell boots or wraps,are not permissibleon the legs of horses except for hoof sole protection as allowed under Section 5.E.2. The leg is defined as all structures above and including the coronet. Section 6.B.2. Soundness 45%: The evaluation of soundness shall include, but not be limit- ed to, the following: lameness (as per the American Association of Equine Practi- tioners’, AAEP, guidelines), heat, filling & pain, tender withers, back or loin, cinch sores, rubs,(including areas covered by any attached strap, keeper, or gai- ter), curbs, splints, ringbone, spavin, sidebone, etc. Way of going will be evalu- ated as a part of soundness. The evalua- tion of way of going shall include, but not be limited to, the following: forging, inter- fering, scalping, over-reaching, continu- ous stumbling, etc. AAEP guidelines: Grade 1: Lameness is difficult to observe and is not consistently apparent, regard- less of circumstance (e.g., under saddle, circling, inclines, hard surface, etc.). Grade 2: Lameness is difficult to observe at a walk or when trotting in a straight line, but consistently apparent under certain circumstance (e.g., weight carrying, cir- cling, inclines, hard surface, etc.). Grade 3: Lameness is consistently observable at a trot under all circumstances. Grade 4: Lameness is obvious at a walk. Grade 5: Lameness produces minimal weight bear- ing in motion and/or rest or a complete inability to move. Section 6.C.1.c. c. Tack and Equipment: The evaluation of tack and equipment shall include, but not be limited to, the following: cleanliness, proper fit, adjustment, repair;adjust- ment, proper fit of any attached strap, keeper, or gaiter; trail gear placement and security. Some points to remember: Leg protection, such as bell boots or wraps, is still not permitted. A “sock” or wrap under a gaiter would be considered leg protection. Such protection should not be necessary if the gaiter or attachment fits properly. Horses should be examined at a trot-out exam as they do the ride. If a horse is ridden shod, he is trotted out in shoes; if he is ridden with pads, he is trotted out in pads; if ridden in boots, he should be trotted out in boots; if he com- petes truly barefoot (without sole protec- tion), he should be trotted out barefoot. If a horse does part of the ride without sole protection and part of the ride with boots, the method used for the trot out would be a judgment call. Judges may, and are encouraged to, check for fit and adjustment as well as rubs or chafes under any boot or its at- tachment. We can anticipate that waiting for a rider to remove the boots may be time-consuming. Unless the rider can quickly remove the boots, the rider should step aside and allow for the next horse to start its exam. At some point during or after the second horse's exam the first, now unbooted, horse can be checked. The vet judge will have to determine what works best for him or her. There should be no scoring ad- vantage for horses that compete bare- foot, nor should there be point deductions for the use of any type of boot or shoe. In essence, the use of sole protection throughout the ride is at the rider's discretion. This last point is important. For instance, if a rider chooses to check in or check out with- out boots and the horse trots out lame in front of the vet judge, that rider may not return with their horse, this time with boots, and ask for a "second chance" soundness exam. This is not within the spirit of the rule. Scoring is dependent upon the rider making the proper choice for his or her horse. The Leadline Spring 2011 10 2011 Ride Schedule May 7 Mt. NATRC E-News is the official electronic communication channel of our organization. E-News is a subscriber-based electronic marketing software from Constant Contact. With E-News, you can receive electronic announcements on upcoming NATRC rides and clinics, new sponsors and special offers, NATRC promotions and contests, and general NATRC information including details on rules changes and proposals as well as regional and national Board minutes in a timely manner by subscribing now. To begin receiving NATRC E-News, simply go to the newly designed NATRC website (www.natrc.org) and click on Members, and then on “Join NATRC E-News” on the left side of your screen. Or click on the “Subscribe to E-News” on the home page of the R1 website. You can choose to receive a variety of different types of announcements. If you would like to send NATRC information to all subscribers or just specific regions, contact Bev Roberts (matefey@gmail.com) or the National office (natrc@natrc.org) for help to put your announcement on the official NATRC electronic communication channel. The Leadline Spring 2011 11 The meeting was held at Livermore, CA, on March 13, 2011 Call to Order:The meeting was called to order by Phil Young, Chairman at 8:03 a.m. Present: Phil Young, Jamie Dieterich, Jim Ferris, Linda Thomason, Angie Meroshne- koff, Steve Meroshnekoff, Stephanie Swain, Laura Harvey. Absent: None. Guests: Sher- ry Lindley-Ferris, Kathy Nixon, Wayne Har- vey, Betty Young, Drina LaRose. Election of Officers:Linda Thomason was nominated for the office of Chairman by Phil Young, seconded by Jamie Dieterich. Stephanie Swain volunteered for the office of Secretary; nominated by Phil Young; sec- onded by Linda Thomason. Phil Young was nominated for the office of Treasurer by Jamie Dieterich, seconded by Linda Thoma- son. All officers elected unanimously. Secretary’s Report: Steve Meroshnekoff moved to approve the Secretary’s Report, including approval of the Minutes of the meetings held 21 February 2010, 27 June 2010, 03 October 2010, and 23 January 2011. Seconded by Phil Young. Approved unanimously. Treasurer’s Report: The savings CD has been closed. Outstanding checks have been written off. These included 2 checks to Juniors and a lost $44.00 check to Phil Young. See written report. The Benefit Ride profited only because of the Raffle money and the Judges donated their fees back. NBOD travel is R1’s biggest expense. Hopefully this will eventually be mitigated by use of Go-To-Meeting for some of the meet- ings, saving NBOD representatives both time and travel expenses. Printing of the Lead Line is the second biggest expense for R1. There are 2 fundraisers per year – Con- vention and the Benefit Ride. 2010 Benefit ride made under $600, Convention made $3191.00. 2012 Convention will not make money for R1 if R2 hosts the national con- vention with AERC. 2011 Proposed budget to be submitted via email by Phil Young. Primary Checking:$23,809.38 Savings (CD):Closed in February Laura Harvey moved to approve the Treas- urer’s Report, Phil Young seconded. Ap- proved unanimously. Committee Reports: Physical Resource Coordinator: Linda Thomason – continuation of bib discussion. Angie brought a sample of a new bib – maximum cost $6.50 per bib. Phil Young moved to approve purchase, Angie Merosh- nekoff seconded. Unanimous agreement. Linda also raised again the idea of charging a refundable deposit to ride managers for using the bibs, to be refunded when the bibs are returned, with a fee for missing or dam- aged bibs. We now have a stand for the projector screen made by Rudy Dezzani. Historian: Kathy Nixon – NATRC pictures were donated at Convention to be put into albums. Wonder Workers: Steve Meroshnekoff – waiting for bids on new chevrons. Will check price of 100 vs. 500. Blue Moon one of possible vendors. Regarding Volunteer Coordination – many volunteers are not signing releases at rides. Ride managers need to be reminded to turn these in. Sherry Lindley-Ferris said that volunteers are being given rider releases at some rides, so they may not be getting filed correctly. Points/Membership: Jim Ferris – currently 47 members have joined/renewed for 2011 with some renewals coming in at R1 Con- vention. This is about the same number as this time last year. Publicity: Linda Thomason – Ride Maga- zine to print pre- and post Convention arti- cles. Clarification between E-blast and E-News: E-Blast is when Jim Ferris sends an email to R1 members; E-News is sub- scriber based National emailing. Jim Ferris to send E-blast regarding subscribing to E- News Benefit Ride: Jean Armer has agreed to have Cowboy Camp at Cache Creek be the R1 2011 Benefit Ride. Discussed awarding ribbons vs. brushes, etc. Consensus to award ribbons, with brushes/etc. to 1 st place finishers. Linda Thomason to place ads in Davis area. Angie working on trails, Jean Armer to send check to National, get insur- ance certificate to BLM. The Leadline: Phil Young – upcoming is- sue will be the Convention issue. Deadline for submissions is 01 April 2011. National BOD: Jamie Dieterich – review of rule changes effective 2011: hoof boots al- lowed, drug rule, rule book to be revised accordingly. Appendices with drug info. available online. Penalty phase of drug rule in process. Should be voted in for 2012. Old drug penalty rule will apply in the interim. 2012 proposed rule changes: 1) Tack –add – rider must use a saddle and headgear with attached reins. R1BOD agrees that this is unnecessary. 2) Membership –2010 voted to refine mem- bership categories and increase dues; as of March 2011 lifetime membership is no long- er offered. Need to update rulebook to agree with vote. 3) Time penalty points – Current rule reads: Time penalty points will be assessed for time violations (See Section 5.J.7). Change to Read: Time penalty points willonly be assessed for time violations (See Section 5.J.7) - R1BOD agrees – too much minutia. Region 1 Website: Angie Meroshnekoff and Jamie Dieterich – Angie received email bill from Debi Carpadus, Region 1 web host for $119. Ride/Clinic Management: Jamie Dieterich – discussion of ride difficulty. 2010 Novice rides were too difficult; need to be easier than CP, not the same. Angie proposed holding a clinic in March/April of 2012 (when R1 Convention would otherwise happen); invite speakers relevant to CTR. Angie to explore possible locations. Outreach Coordinator: Linda Thomason – Linda will only be at about 3 rides this year due to her work schedule. She needs someone to act as New Rider Outreach at rides, giving out new rider bags, which would include copies of the new boot rule and information on the drug rule. Trails Advocacy Coordinator:Judy Eth- eridge – no report. Bylaws: Jim Ferris, Jamie Dieterich, Phil Young, Nancy Kasovich – only proposed changes would be that which is necessary to do the proposed new incorporation and disposition of property owned by R1. Judging Issues: Jamie Dieterich, Betty Young – Jamie stated that riders are asking for more informative comments from the judges. Old Business: T-Shirt/Sweatshirt Sales: Angie Merosh- nekoff – Betty sold 8 T-shirts at Convention. Linda will keep the rest and get them to rides to be offered for sale. NATRC Computers: Jim Ferris – work in progress. Ride Calendar: Jamie Dieterich – Hoof Trek Monterey and Harvey Bear Ranch rides have been cancelled. Georgetown ride is a go. Change of location for R1 BOD meetings: Angie unable to find a suitable location in Williams or Colusa. Will check the Wood- land area. R1 Corporate and Non-profit status: Phil Young and Jamie Dieterich are working to- gether on the 37 page application form. Jim Ferris got a book on the subject. Work in progress. 2011 R1 Mini Convention: Linda Thoma- son – report at next meeting. New Business: Merge R1 Awards convention with the 2012 National Convention? R1BOD approved unanimously. Next Meeting:Date: 22 May 2011. Time: 8:00 a.m. Location: Cowboy Camp ride, Cache Creek Wilderness. Tentative meeting dates/locations: 25 September 2011 @ 9:30 a.m., location TBD (Woodland or Jim Ferris’ home). ??January 2012 via Go-To- Meeting Close of Meeting: Motion to adjourn by Stephanie; seconded by Phil; all agreed. The Leadline Spring 2013 1 In This Issue.... Spring 2013 www.natrcregion1.org Doug was born December 3, 1955 in Port Huron, Michigan to a proud Richard Lee McCue and Helen Mae Smith. He was the second oldest of their four children, including an older brother, Richard, and younger siblings, Nathan and Crystal. When Doug was four years old, the family lost their father to a construction accident. On April 13, 1962 Helen married Emerson Bruce Brown, whom she had known from church camps years before. Their marriage added Carolyn, Sandra, and Tammy to their family. The whole family loved to attend church and summer camps regularly. Doug was incredibly smart and he loved to read, easily retaining what he read. When he was younger, Helen would often pick up on a pattern — when there were chores to be done, she secretly thought that Doug would purposefully misbehave so that he could be sent to his room. When she would check on him later, he would either be reading the encyclopedia or the dictionary. Doug always loved a good party. Once, when he was going home from a High School camp, Doug told his parents he had invited some of the kids to their home the next weekend for a pool party. When asked how many he invited, he said everyone who was there. Bruce and Helen weren't sure how many would show, but there were at least 70 kids at the camp. The next Saturday night when Bruce came home from work, he was greeted with loud music, a yard full of cars, and a house and backyard full of about 100 kids! Kids in the pool, kids on the pool deck, sliding down the pool slide on top of one another, and several guys trying to throw one of their friends in the pool. On the patio, there was a small band with huge speakers blaring music. What a night! While he was attending Graceland College in Lamoni, Iowa, Doug met Tamara Ann Martin. After Tammy graduated, Doug went west to California with Tammy and never looked back. The two were married on June 17, 1978 in Walnut Creek, California. In 1979, Doug and Tammy purchased a condo in Concord, near the drive-in movies. From the second bedroom window, they could see the shows; however, lip reading was needed to fully enjoy the pictures. They said goodbye to their personal movie theatre in 1981 when they purchased a duplex in San Ramon, California. Then, in 1983, Doug and Tammy purchased their current home in San Ramon. Over the years, Tammy always owned a horse and rode often. After just a few years of marriage, Doug decided that he needed to buy a horse and learn to ride so that he could spend more time with his wife. Doug pur- chased Shah, an Arabian horse who instantly became a part of the family. Doug sometimes rode Shah on Mount Diablo without Tammy, and sometimes would get lost. Doug would let Shah decide which way to go when they came to a fork in the road, and Shah would always bring Doug back home in time for dinner. Doug worked for 30 years at Allied Sy- sco, a food distribution company, in the ware- house as a forklift operator. In 1987, on December 16, Doug and Tammy were blessed with their son, Jeffrey Martin Brown. Doug was incredibly proud of his amazing son. In Memoriam: Douglas Norman Brown 12/3/1955 — 3/28/2013 Doug Brown and National Treasure Photo by Jamie Dieiterich ...continued on Page 2 Chairman’s Corner...................3 From The Editor.......................4 Region 1 Year-End Awards.....5 Rules Versus Expectations......9 National Meeting Highlights.........................10 3 Saddle Stretches................11 Region 1 Ride Calendar........13 Endurance Riders’ Secrets....14 Region 1 BOD Meeting Minutes...............16 Region 1 General Membership Meeting Minutes........................17 NATRC Membership Form....18 Region 1 has a Facebook Page! Find us at NATRC Region 1 The Leadline Spring 2013 2 Doug taught Jeff how to shoot, how be a marksman. Over the years, shooting was something that they would do together. Doug would often invite people to go with them, to use shooting as an excuse to buy everyone lunch. Doug and Jeff sometimes went hunting, and Tammy was quite pleased that they always came home empty handed. On March 26, 2012, Jeff married Han- nah Gruver, with Tammy and Doug as well as Bruce and Helen in attendance. Doug and Tammy's love of horses nev- er disappeared. Through their twenties, they rode horses in competitive trail riding events with the North American Trail Ride Confer- ence (NATRC). Doug rode Shah, an Arabi- an gelding for 25 years, doing a bit of endurance in the 80's, and pleasure riding with Tammy. In 2009, he won top honors in NATRC Region 1 Novice Heavyweight, rid- ing Jazz, an Arabian-Saddlebred cross geld- ing. In 2011 he rode a borrowed horse, Blue Camel, aka Chunkey Monkey, a Foxtrotter gelding. In 2012, he rode his new horse, National Treasure, an Arabian gelding, in the Competitive Pleasure Division. Doug and National Treasure competed very suc- cessfully in the Open Division of Arizona’s Triple Crown series of three rides in early 2013. Doug exemplified Christ’s love toward everyone he met. If he saw a need, he did his best to lend a hand, whether it was helping a neighbor replace his lawn, hang- ing a picture, hauling firewood—nothing was off limits. He volunteered at church functions and attended a small group Bible study and spread the love of Jesus through his words and actions. Doug truly fulfilled the purpose for which he was created! The family of Douglas Norman Brown wishes to thank each of you for your prayers, support, and friendship. Blessings to you. Dear Doug, I remember the exact moment you came into my life. We were navigating a hair-pin turn at Round Valley, and I wasn’t paying attention to anything except my horse. You chatted with Bailey Dorsey just ahead of me, but worry kept me intensely focused on every step my horse took. He’d checked in lame; the vet al- lowed us to start, but I doubted we’d finish the ride. As it turned out, so did you. So did Bailey. So did anyone with a lick of sense. I didn’t blame any of you a bit. My horse had used his saddle for a hula hoop at the last ride, leaving him with a limp, me waiting for the rescue wagon, and Bailey stranded without a riding partner. You were really riding with Bailey that day, in the anticipated event that I pulled. I was just glad to be allowed to tag along. As we completed the turn, I over-heard my name and looked up. You blinked before smiling sheepishly. “I’m sorry, Laura. I didn’t know you were so close. I didn’t mean to be talking out of school.” It took me a minute to realize you must have said something I might interpret negatively about me, or my horse, and the ap- propriateness of us being out on the trail. “Uh, that’s all right.” I hadn’t actually heard what you said. Knowing you, it was probably very mild, and far better than I deserved. Whatever it was, I figured you were right. You flashed a grin at me and we continued on. In that moment, I understood a dozen things about you. At any given mo- ment, you had one eye on the welfare of the horses--all the horses, not just your own. You kept the other eye on the welfare of the riders. Your own personal stake in the competition ranked near the bottom of the stack. And if you felt you’d mis- stepped or misspoken, you’d apologize before anyone realized anything was amiss. I thought of you as my big brother on the trail. I felt secure knowing you were there, even if I couldn’t see you, since you had the knack of appearing at just the right moment. When I was so exhausted at check-out that I couldn’t face walking down the stinkin’ hill at Mt. Diablo one more time to fetch a different rope, you put one in my hand. When my horse was in crisis, you had a bucket and sponge at the ready. You helped me get Tango into the creek at Round Valley. When a horse got tangled in a rope, you cut the line before I even thought of a knife. When we encountered a locked gate, you fetched your Leatherman tool. You stuffed Kaysha and baby Lorelei in your camper with the air conditioner during a freak heat wave. (You stuffed me in, too.) You offered shade, cold tea, guacamole, and whatever you happened to be cooking. My waistline will miss you at future rides. I don’t really believe in angels, but if there is such a creature, it surely could not be any more great-hearted, generous, or timely than you. It certainly could not be any more humble. If there is any chance of the departed looking after the living, I can’t imagine anyone more likely to be looking after me—after all of us— on the trail. I think of you every time I navigate that turn at Round Valley. I will continue to think of you as just out of sight. I will try to keep one eye on all the horses, and the other on all the riders. I’ll pack guacamole when I load up for a ride. I hope, in time, to be half as great-hearted as you. If I get even that far, I’ll be pretty awe- some. See you on the trail, brother. Laura The following letter was written by Laura Harvey. Administrator Laurie DiNatale 303.688.1677 natrc@natrc.org Hoof Print Editor Laurie Knuutila wildrosepht@yahoo.com NATRC Home Page: www.NATRC.org CTR Listserve info:jim.c.ferris@gmail.com See www.natrc.org for National Board, Committees, and Secretaries Linda Thomason 510.651.9470 linda.thomason@comcast.net Chairman’s Corner Well friends, we survived the winter snow, rain, and mud once again and the ride season is finally here! Time to get out on the trails and enjoy Spring. First off, I’d like to welcome Joe Pimentel to the Board of Directors (BOD) for Region 1. There were 3 seats open this year. Gene Boicelli and Stephanie Swain were re-elected and this is Joe’s first venture into the BOD. Many of you know Joe, who competes with his horse “Kodi.” So when you see Joe or any of the other BOD members, talk to them, provide them with your feedback/ideas and thank them for taking the time to represent YOU on the Board of Directors. The Board meets several times per year to discuss issues important to Region 1 and discuss feedback from members. This season, I’m again asking each and every one of you to get the word out about NATRC and competitive trail riding. We need to increase member- ship to continue successfully putting on events. Support your Region and try to bring in at least one new rider this season. At the Round Valley Ride, there is a 25% discount being offered for each first time competitor a current NATRC member brings to the ride. How can you pass up a deal like that? Know someone who may be too shy to enter an NATRC ride before they know what they’re getting into? This year there will be a ride clinic held in conjunction with the Cowboy Camp Ride in May. I encourage all members to notify friends and fellow riders who may have expressed an interest in CTR. This is an opportunity to camp with the Cowboy Camp competitors and participate in a CTR clinic without the stress of being in competition. Clinic participants will learn what NATRC competitive trail riding is all about. They will learn how to present to judges, what the judges are looking for, how to pace themselves and time their ride, how they will be scored and the many other aspects of riding in a competitive trail ride. So this is a call to all members: contact fellow riders who might be interested in a fun weekend of horse camping, riding the trails at Cowboy Camp and learning about compet- itive trail riding. It’s a great opportunity to bring riders into the sport so support Region 1 and help advertise this event. This past March, our NATRC family lost a wonderful member, Doug Brown. Those of us who had the privilege to know or ride with Doug will miss his smiling face coming over the hill. I never knew Doug to say an unkind word about anyone. At the rides he maintained the most positive attitude and kept this good attitude regardless if he had to pull, got lost or was just plumb tired. Thanks to everyone who made it to the memorial and for the cards and e-mails to support Tammy. It’s so very sad to lose someone but we are all better for having known Doug, as a fellow competitor, rider, NATRC supporter and just all around nice guy! If you have stories you’d like to share about Doug, please submit them on the memorial page on the Region 1 website. Happy trails, Doug, you will be missed. As we start the ride season, a reminder to all about how much work is involved with putting on rides. Even if the ride isn’t quite up to your expecta- tions, please make suggestions for improving it in a sincere and kind-hearted manner. None of us are professional event planners. We’re all VOLUNTEERS and things don’t always go as expected at the rides. So take the time to thank those volunteers, ride managers, P&R teams, secretaries, cooks—all the unpaid heroes who make it possible for you to ride great trails and have a fun weekend competing. And if for some reason you can’t ride, please volunteer to help out at a ride. It’s a sport, folks, so let’s have FUN and work together to make it a great ride season. See you at the rides Linda Thomason Region 1 Chairperson 209.537-9355 or209.652-1091(cell) Joe Pimentel lecacyranch1@aol.com pbyoung@sunset.net Phil Young The Leadline Spring 2013 4 From The Editor... Spring has arrived, sort of, here in the Far North. We are finally beginning to see a few water puddles from snow- melt, and a few migrating birds are re- turning. But spring has definitely taken its time arriving this year! I have been riding, but it’s been limited, due to deep snow and slick footing. I’m looking for- ward to more saddle time in the next few weeks as the weather warms up. This issue of the Leadline includes the lists of winners for the 2012 season. A big Congratulations and a hearty Well Done to all of the Region 1 riders who made the lists! These lists represent many, many hours of hard work and dedication to an awesome sport! I’ve included some articles that I hope you will find helpful. If you have any suggestions on subjects you’d like to see covered, or ideas for newsletter content, please contact me. Of course, I always need ride stories and pictures to help make the ride results come to life! So as the ride season begins, please think of me and write me a story about your adventures or shoot some pictures at the various Region 1 rides. I’m waiting to hear from you! The two individuals in the photo below have been frequenting my prop- erty for most of the winter. My horses have grown used to them walking around the perimeter of the paddocks and through the woods, and they hardly even warrant a stare anymore! Take care, and may your riding season be all you hope it will be! Laurie Knuutila A cow moose and her calf pay a visit to my garden area and snack on frozen garden leftovers. Photo by Tony Knuutila Attention All 4-H Horse Members! To encourage participation by 4H members, NATRC offers a 4H Junior Rider Year End High Point Award. ∑The award goes to the high point rider and high point horseregard- less of the rider’s division. ∑The award is a self-nominating award. The nomination must occur before the end of the ride year, which is the second Sunday in November. This year, it will be November 10, 2013. The rider must (1) be a member of NATRC (2) send Laurie DiNatale an email (natrc@natrc.org) stating that they are a 4H member and (3 have their 4H leader verify that they are a member with an email to Laurie DiNatale (natrc@natrc.org). In a number of states (Colorado is one example), 4H now recognizes competitive trail as an activity that qualifies as a 4H member’s project. Questions? Issues contain updated information from Region One committees, ride results, point standings, minutes of board meetings, and the current Region One ride schedule.
